---
title: "Workflow Management"
subtitle: "Automating multi-step computational pipelines"
author: "Samuel Pawel, Peter Degen, Fabio Molo, Gorka Fraga González"
date-format: long
preview-links: true
include-in-header:
  - text: |
      <style>
      #title-slide .title {
        font-size: 1.8em;
        color: #29487d;
      }
      </style>
format:
  revealjs:
    menu: true
    pdf: true
    code-line-numbers: false
    smaller: true
    fig-align: left
    logo: "img/uzh_logo_d_pos_web_assoc.jpg"
    embed-resources: true
    progress: true
    slide-number: true
    footer: "Center for Reproducible Science and Research Synthesis | [Home](../../index.qmd)"
    title-slide-attributes:
      data-background-size: contain
      data-background-position: bottom
      data-background-opacity: "0.8"
bibliography: references.bib
nocite: |
  @Peikert2021
---

## The problem: multi-step pipelines 🤠

::: incremental
-   A typical research project has **multiple scripts** that must run in order:

    -   `download.py` → `clean.R` → `analyse.py` → `figures.R` → `paper.qmd`

-   How to know **which files to run in which order**?

-   How to avoid **re-running everything** when only one file changed?

-   How to make the pipeline **self-documenting and shareable**?

. . .

**Solution: workflow management tools (e.g., Make, Snakemake, Nextflow, ...)**
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Automation with Make

-   Tool for managing **computational recipes**
-   Around since the 1976s
-   Originally designed for software compilation
-   **Automating multiple steps** into one

. . .

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
> Arrabbiata sauce, or sugo all'arrabbiata in Italian, is a spicy sauce for pasta made from garlic, tomatoes, and dried red chili peppers cooked in olive oil.

[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arrabbiata_sauce](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arrabbiata_sauce){style="font-size:0.7em"}
:::

:::: {.column width="50%"}
::: fragment
Makefile

``` makefile
# target : dependencies
# recipe (indented with TAB)
arrabiata.pdf: arrabiata.qmd sauce.csv pasta.R
  quarto render arrabiata.qmd

sauce.csv: cook.R tomatoes.zip aromatics.yaml
  R -e "source(cook.R)"

aromatics.yaml: sizzle.py garlic.txt chili.json
  python sizzle.py garlic.txt
  python sizzle.py chili.json
```

Terminal

``` bash
make arrabiata.pdf # execute recipe
```
:::
::::
::::::

::: fragment
![](img/Make-diagram.png){fig-align="center" width="600"}
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## How Make works

::: incremental
-   A **Makefile** defines **targets**, **dependencies**, and **recipes**

-   Make checks **timestamps**: only re-runs a target if a dependency is newer

-   **Dependency graph** ensures correct execution order automatically

-   Running `make` from the terminal executes the minimal set of steps needed
:::

::: fragment
``` makefile
# Pattern: target: dependencies
#              recipe (must be indented with a TAB)

report.html: analysis.R data.csv paper.qmd
    Rscript analysis.R
    quarto render paper.qmd
```
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## A minimal research Makefile

``` makefile
# Final target
all: paper.pdf

# Render the paper (depends on cleaned data and figures)
paper.pdf: paper.qmd results/estimates.rds figures/fig1.png
    quarto render paper.qmd --to pdf

# Run statistical analysis
results/estimates.rds: R/analyse.R data/clean.csv
    Rscript R/analyse.R

# Generate figure 1
figures/fig1.png: R/figures.R results/estimates.rds
    Rscript R/figures.R

# Clean raw data
data/clean.csv: R/clean.R data/raw.csv
    Rscript R/clean.R

# Remove all generated files
clean:
    rm -rf paper.pdf results/ figures/
```

\$

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Snakemake

[https://snakemake.readthedocs.io](https://snakemake.readthedocs.io){style="font-size:0.7em"}

::: incremental
-   **Python-flavoured syntax**: rules look like Python
-   **Content hashes**: robust to `touch`, `git checkout`, file copies, etc. (only re-run if content changed, not timestamp)
-   **Wildcards**: one rule applies to many samples (`samples/{name}.csv` fans out automatically)
-   Per-rule **conda env or container**: each step pinned to its own environment
-   **Cluster / cloud execution**: built in: SLURM, AWS, Kubernetes (same workflow, different backend)
-   Auto-generated **DAG diagrams** and HTML reports
:::

::: fragment
``` python
rule report:
    input:  "work/3_processed.csv"
    output: "results/4_report.html"
    shell:  "quarto render 4_report.qmd"
```
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Nextflow — pipelines as dataflow

[https://www.nextflow.io](https://www.nextflow.io){style="font-size:0.7em"}

::: incremental
-   **Dataflow paradigm**: data flows through *channels* into *processes* — the DAG emerges from how channels connect
-   **First-class containers**: each process declares its Docker / Singularity image
-   **`-resume`** uses content hashes to skip steps whose inputs haven't changed
-   **Cloud-native execution**: AWS Batch, Azure, Google Cloud, Kubernetes
-   **nf-core**: 100+ peer-reviewed, community-maintained pipelines (heavily used in bioinformatics)
:::

::: fragment
``` groovy
workflow {
  derived = PY_DERIVE( file('example_data.csv') )
  R_SUMMARY( derived )                       // 'derived' wires step 1 → step 2
}

process PY_DERIVE {
  input:  path "example_data.csv"
  output: path "1_derived.csv"
  script: "python 1_derive.py example_data.csv 1_derived.csv"
}

process R_SUMMARY {
  input:  path "1_derived.csv"
  output: path "2_summary.txt"
  script: "Rscript 2_summary.R 1_derived.csv 2_summary.txt"
}
```
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## CRS Primer on Workflow Management

:::: columns
::: {.column width="45%"}
![<https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.15792190>](img/primer-make.png)
:::
::::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Additional Resources

-   GNU Make manual: <https://www.gnu.org/software/make/manual/>
-   The `targets` R package book: <https://books.ropensci.org/targets/>
-   Snakemake documentation: <https://snakemake.readthedocs.io>
-   Nextflow documentation: <https://www.nextflow.io/docs/latest/>
-   nf-core — community Nextflow pipelines: <https://nf-co.re>
-   The Turing Way — Reproducible Pipelines: <https://the-turing-way.netlify.app/reproducible-research/make>
-   @Peikert2021: a full reproducible workflow combining Make, Git, and Docker

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## References {.smaller}
